title: The compounding cost of fees
subtitle: A 1% annual fee sounds small. Over 30 years, it is anything but.

body.content: Fees are the one thing in investing you can control completely. You can't control what the market does. You can't guarantee returns. But you can guarantee that you're not paying more than you need to. Every dollar you pay in fees is a dollar not compounding for you. At small percentages, this sounds academic. At scale, over decades, it is ruinous.
Expense ratios: the most important fee

The expense ratio is the annual percentage of your investment taken by the fund to cover operating costs. It comes out automatically — you never write a check, you never see an invoice. On Vanguard's total market index fund (VTI), the expense ratio is 0.03%. On an average actively managed fund, it's around 0.8-1.0%. On some annuities or insurance products marketed as investments, it can be 2-3%. The fee looks small. The compounding difference is not.

body.label: The math
body.content: $100,000 invested for 30 years at 7% gross annual return: at 0.03% expense ratio, you end with approximately $741,000. At 1.0% expense ratio, you end with approximately $574,000. At 2.0%, approximately $432,000. The 2% fee scenario gives you $309,000 less than the 0.03% scenario. On the same gross return. That's the compounding cost of fees.

items: Front-end loads: some mutual funds charge 3-5% upfront just to buy them. Avoid these entirely.
Advisory fees: financial advisors typically charge 0.5-1.5% of assets under management per year. Sometimes worth it; often not.
Trading costs: commissions are now zero at most major brokerages for stocks and ETFs. But frequent trading creates bid-ask spread costs.
Tax drag: realizing gains frequently creates tax events that reduce net return. Index funds have low turnover and minimal tax drag.
Wrap fees: all-in fees on managed accounts that bundle multiple charges — read the fine print.

body.content: When a 1% advisory fee is worth it

A good financial advisor can provide real value: comprehensive financial planning, tax optimization, behavioral coaching during crashes, estate planning coordination. If an advisor's guidance prevents one panic-sell during a bear market, they've likely earned their fee many times over. The question isn't whether the fee is zero — it's whether the value exceeds the cost. An advisor charging 1% who keeps you from selling in 2020's crash saved you 30%. Worth it. An advisor charging 1% who puts you in high-fee mutual funds and rebalances once a year? Less clear.

In a taxable account, every time you sell a winning investment, you owe capital gains tax. Active funds with high turnover — buying and selling frequently — create constant taxable events, even for investors who did nothing. Index funds have low turnover (they only trade when the index changes) and generate far fewer taxable events. This tax efficiency is a real, if invisible, advantage — it shows up in your net return but never appears on a fee schedule.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: Rule of thumb: keep total investment costs below 0.20% per year. That's achievable with index funds at any major brokerage. Anything above 0.50% should require a clear explanation of what you're getting for the extra cost. Anything above 1% better be delivering exceptional value — and most of the time, it isn't.

Fees compound against you just as returns compound for you. A 1% expense ratio costs roughly $167,000 on a $100,000 investment over 30 years compared to 0.03%. Keep total costs under 0.20%. Use index funds. Consider advisors only when their specific value clearly exceeds their cost.
